The rather tepid market reception to Amazon’s new bond issuance brings a question back to the forefront (as discussed a few weeks ago; see link below): the absorption capacity of international bond investors.
Markets are being asked to digest massive supply from governments, traditional corporates, and, most notably, tech hyperscalers.
To put this in perspective, hyperscalers alone have issued over $300 billion in debt year-to-date. That is double last year’s pace, and the trajectory is only expected to steepen.
https://t.co/oYNTskYHBV
#economy #bonds #markets @FT

tweets.text:
Today we get the Federal Reserve minutes from the June 16-17 FOMC meeting.
While inevitably partial and narrow, they will offer the first glimpse into the committee's deliberations under Chair Kevin Warsh.
Recall that the new Chair opted out of the June "dot plot," consistent with his (and others') distaste for conventional forward guidance. With that communication tool likely to be further sidelined in the months to come, markets will carefully parse the minutes, particularly in light of the balance between FOMC members worried about sticky inflation and those more focused on potential labor market vulnerabilities.
#economy #markets #federalreserve

The total RWA market cap is around ~$28.3B on @DefiLlama, but only about ~$3.23B is active in DeFi.

That means only ~11% of onchain RWA value is actually being used inside DeFi right now.

So IMO, the current state of RWA is pretty clear: the market has already proven that real-world assets can be brought onchain, but tokenizing an asset is not the same thing as making it useful for DeFi strategies.

You can see this in the numbers too.

Some of the biggest RWA products have billions in onchain market cap, but very little DeFi utilization, and to me that means that the next phase is going to be different.

Especially now that tokenized corporate credit and tokenized private equity have both crossed $1B in distributed value.

So I'm wondering whether they become active financial primitives, or just tokenized wrappers people just hold onchain.

I can't stress enough how much liquidity fragmentation is hurting crypto in the long run.

I'm always on the hunt for new projects that are trying to make markets more active, easier to use, and less fragmented.

So let me explain why @WaterX_app is the latest project I put on my watchlist:

They launched on @SuiNetwork earlier this month with ~30 perp markets and are backed by Sui's Moonshots Program.

The interesting part: they're building an AI-native trading platform that combines perps and their newly launched Beta prediction market in one place.

And I like that WaterX Predict Beta has a "swipe to predict" feature, which simplifies the process with a mechanic most people already understand  (like swiping left or right on a dating app).

tweets.text:
Cheaper intelligence was supposed to end the AI trade.

The bear case rested on compute staying scarce. DeepSeek cracked that assumption by matching frontier output at a fraction of the cost. Cheap intelligence meant the money pouring into capacity was about to be wasted.

However, the opposite happened.

The reason starts with scaling. More compute makes it possible to run more capable models. So when cost falls the same spend buys capability the old models never had.

Jevons paradox does the rest. Each decline in cost makes new use cases viable, so total spending rises even as the cost per unit falls.

That accounts for demand, but the bubble case rests on supply. AI compute doesn't flood the market the way telecom did in the 1990s, because the constraints are physical: fab capacity, advanced memory, power, and the EUV behind them. Each takes years to expand. Supply stays scarce while demand climbs.

If that holds, the market oversupply that people are expecting may not form. What looks like a bubble could just be demand meeting a hard limit on supply.

(Original content provided by @PonderingDurian at Delphi Ventures)

tweets.text:
Arcium is the encrypted compute layer for blockchains.

Everything on a public blockchain is permanently visible. That works for speculation but rules out anything that has to keep a secret while it runs. A sealed-bid auction collapses once the bids are visible. An order gets front-run once its size is public. None of this can move onchain because the transaction exposes the secret the moment it is visible.

@Arcium separates verification from execution. The outcome stays public and verifiable, while the logic that has to stay hidden runs privately across a cluster of nodes where no single node reconstructs the full data. Arcium's live backend (Cerberus) keeps the result correct as long as one honest node remains. Settlement happens onchain, but the inputs stay private.

This opens up a new design space for public blockchains. A launchpad can launch as a sealed-bid auction where no bidder sees the other bids until it closes. A DEX can process an order privately and reduce the surface for MEV. A company can run payroll onchain without exposing its pay structure.

Privacy-oriented DeFi has tried to address this by walling off everything, but this came at the cost of composability and fragmented liquidity. Arcium keeps settlement public and composable while the sensitive logic stays hidden. It lets a public chain keep a secret.

tweets.text:
One of my bull cases for commodities has been the expected stock piling I expect to see of a variety of things. A story I read on Bloomberg this morning, “The US Department of Defense is buying lithium for its strategic stockpiles as the nation ramps up efforts to reduce supply risks for critical minerals. The Defense Logistics Agency is seeking offers for almost 36 million pounds, about 16,000 tons, of battery grade lithium carbonate over the next five years in a contract worth as much as $300 million, according to a tender document published on a US government website dated July 2.”

Yesterday, I read this on Reuters, “Germany’s Economy Ministry is drawing up plans for a state-owned strategic gas reserve to be used in emergencies...The reserve would hold around 24 terawatt-hours (TWh) of gas, equivalent to just under 10% of Germany’s total gas storage capacity, and would be financed through a levy on gas consumers, the ministry said. The reserve is intended to protect against extreme situation, such as sabotage of critical energy infrastructure or a severe global gas storage.”

I expect to hear a lot more of these type stories in the months to come.

tweets.text:
Spent my morning reading this recent speech from @SecScottBessent. It lays out a new and defining vision for America's role in the economy for the next 100 years. @elerianm calls it a "remarkably important speech."

Bessent organizes his vision around five principles. Principle 3 is, "America will write the rules of the next economy." He gives one example of what this means:

"Digital assets, stablecoins, tokenization, and new payment systems will help to shape the future of money. The United States should not consign itself to the sidelines while that future is built elsewhere."

If you've wondered how committed Washington is to making crypto succeed in the US, that tells you something.

tweets.text:
first, for ethereum to capture mev directly would not change this dynamic at all. for apps it doesnt matter if the leak-through mev is captured by validators or eth holders - they will try to internalize it nontheless. depending on the complexity and how fast uncaptured mev compresses, mev internalization might not be worth it at all for ethereum.

second, not sure what you are talking about re staking dynamics. congestion fees are presently 100% burned and not paid out to validators, and mev is also a tiny (<5%) part of overall validator rewards. so one takeaway from my thread should be that increasing adoption will not save ethereum validators/stakers, should issuance be lowered. this issuance is all they get.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
@hasufl Thank you for going into more detail on this. It makes complete sense to me now to think of priority fees more as an inefficiency leak in the system.

AFAIK, there are research efforts for Ethereum to equalize and capture MEV directly, though. Wouldn't that change this dynamic?
